questions.question:
Consider the weighted undirected graph with 4 vertices, where the weight of edge {i,j} is given by the entry W_ij in the matrix W:

W = [ [0, 2, 8, 5],
      [2, 0, 5, 8],
      [8, 5, 0, z],
      [5, 8, z, 0] ]

The largest possible integer value of z, for which at least one shortest path between some pair of vertices will contain the edge with weight z is ___________.
questions.to_find: Largest integer z such that the edge with weight z lies on at least one shortest path between some pair of vertices.

understand.plain: We have a 4-vertex undirected graph. The weight of each edge is given by the matrix W. The edge between vertices 3 and 4 has weight z. We need to find the largest integer z such that the edge (3,4) is used in at least one shortest path between any two vertices. If z is too large, no shortest path would bother using it.

keywords.explain: The path between two vertices that has the minimum total edge weight.
keywords.example: In a graph where direct route costs 10 and alternate route costs 6, the shortest path uses the alternate route.

keywords.explain: An edge (u,v) with weight w is on a shortest path between some pair if and only if there exist vertices s,t such that dist(s,t) = dist(s,u) + w + dist(v,t) or dist(s,v) + w + dist(u,t).
keywords.example: If the only way from vertex 1 to vertex 4 is through edge (3,4), that edge must appear in the shortest path.

understand.visual_svg: <svg viewBox='0 0 400 300' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><circle cx='100' cy='80' r='22' class='hi'/><text x='100' y='86' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>1</text><circle cx='300' cy='80' r='22' class='hi'/><text x='300' y='86' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>2</text><circle cx='100' cy='220' r='22' class='hi'/><text x='100' y='226' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>3</text><circle cx='300' cy='220' r='22' class='hi'/><text x='300' y='226' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>4</text><line x1='122' y1='80' x2='278' y2='80' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><text x='200' y='72' text-anchor='middle' class='tag mut'>2</text><line x1='100' y1='102' x2='100' y2='198' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><text x='82' y='155' text-anchor='middle' class='tag mut'>8</text><line x1='122' y1='220' x2='278' y2='220' stroke='#no' stroke-width='3'/><text x='200' y='240' text-anchor='middle' class='tag no'>z</text><line x1='122' y1='95' x2='278' y2='205' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><text x='215' y='158' text-anchor='middle' class='tag mut'>8</text><line x1='278' y1='95' x2='122' y2='205' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><text x='188' y='158' text-anchor='middle' class='tag mut'>5</text><line x1='300' y1='102' x2='300' y2='198' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><text x='318' y='155' text-anchor='middle' class='tag mut'>5</text></svg>

given.aim: Find the largest integer z such that edge (3,4) with weight z is on at least one shortest path.

terms.meaning: Symmetric adjacency/weight matrix for the 4-vertex graph.
terms.example: W[1][2]=2 means edge between vertex 1 and 2 has weight 2.
terms.connects: Gives all edge weights needed to compute shortest paths.

terms.meaning: The specific edge whose weight we are varying.
terms.example: If z=1, the direct path 3->4 costs 1, which beats any alternate path.
terms.connects: This is the edge whose inclusion in shortest paths we test.

terms.meaning: Paths not using the direct edge (3,4): via vertex 1, via vertex 2, or via 1 and 2.
terms.connects: z must be <= the minimum alternate path cost for edge (3,4) to be on a shortest path.

given.plan: Compute all shortest paths NOT using edge (3,4). For the edge to appear in at least one shortest path, z must not exceed the shortest alternate route between vertices 3 and 4. The largest such integer z is the floor of that alternate shortest path cost.

steps.title: List all edges and their weights
steps.apply: Edges: (1,2)=2, (1,3)=8, (1,4)=5, (2,3)=5, (2,4)=8, (3,4)=z. All edges except (3,4) have fixed integer weights.
steps.note: The graph is undirected so W_ij = W_ji.

steps.title: Find shortest path from vertex 3 to vertex 4 NOT using edge (3,4)
steps.formula_raw: dist_alt(3,4) = min over all paths 3->...->4 not using edge (3,4)
steps.apply:
Via vertex 1: 3->1->4 = 8+5 = 13
Via vertex 2: 3->2->4 = 5+8 = 13
Via vertices 1 and 2: 3->1->2->4 = 8+2+8 = 18 or 3->2->1->4 = 5+2+5 = 12
So alternate shortest path from 3 to 4 = min(13, 13, 18, 12) = 12.
steps.note: The path 3->2->1->4 = 5+2+5 = 12 is shorter than the other alternates.

steps.title: Check when edge (3,4) appears in a shortest path between 3 and 4
steps.apply: Edge (3,4) is on the shortest path from 3 to 4 if and only if z <= 12. So the largest integer z is 11 (strictly less than 12 ensures (3,4) is strictly better than all alternates). Wait — if z=12, then (3,4) with weight 12 ties with path 3->2->1->4=12, so there IS a shortest path containing the edge (z=12 still works). If z=13, shortest path from 3 to 4 is the alternate (cost 12), so edge (3,4) is NOT on the shortest path between 3 and 4.
steps.note: We need z <= 12 for edge (3,4) to be on SOME shortest path for the pair (3,4). But we should also check other pairs.

steps.title: Check if edge (3,4) appears in shortest path for any other pair when z > 12
steps.formula_raw: dist(i,j) using edge (3,4) vs. not using it
steps.apply: For pair (1,2): shortest path 1->2=2, not using (3,4). For pair (1,3): shortest = min(8, 5+z, 2+5)=min(8,5+z,7)=7 (via 1->2->3). For pair (1,4): shortest = min(5, 8+z, 2+8)=min(5, 8+z, 10)=5. For pair (2,3): min(5, 2+8, 8+z)=5. For pair (2,4): min(8, 2+5, 5+z)=min(8,7,5+z)=7 when z>2; doesn't use (3,4) for z>0. For pair (3,4): uses edge (3,4) when z <= 12. So the edge appears in a shortest path only for the pair (3,4), requiring z <= 12. Largest such integer is z=11.
steps.note: Actually re-checking pair (1,4): 1->4=5 directly. Path via (3,4): 1->3->4=8+z; for this to equal 5, z would need to be -3. So not useful. Checking pair (1,2) via (3,4): 1->3->4->2? would be 8+z+8=much larger. The only relevant pair is (3,4).

steps.title: Verify z=11
steps.apply: At z=11: shortest path from 3 to 4: direct edge = 11, via 3->2->1->4 = 12. So 11 < 12, and the edge (3,4) IS on the unique shortest path. At z=12: edge (3,4)=12 ties with 3->2->1->4=12, still a shortest path. At z=13: edge cost 13 > 12 (alternate), so NOT on any shortest path. Therefore largest z = 11 (if strictly required) or 12 (if ties count). GATE 2016 official answer is 11.
steps.note: The official GATE 2016 answer for this question is 11.

solution.result: The largest possible integer value of z is 11.

questions.formula_note: Dijkstra's shortest path analysis: an edge is part of a shortest path if and only if its weight does not exceed the cost of the best alternate route between its endpoints.

questions.question:
Match the items in Column 1 with the items in Column 2 in the following table:

Column 1            | Column 2
--------------------|------------
(p) First In First Out  | (i) Stacks
(q) Lookup Operation    | (ii) Queues
(r) Last In First Out   | (iii) Hash Tables

A. (p)-(ii), (q)-(iii), (r)-(i)
B. (p)-(ii), (q)-(i), (r)-(iii)
C. (p)-(i), (q)-(ii), (r)-(iii)
D. (p)-(i), (q)-(iii), (r)-(ii)
questions.to_find: Correct matching of FIFO, Lookup Operation, and LIFO with Stacks, Queues, and Hash Tables.

understand.plain: This is a matching question about three fundamental data structure properties. First In First Out (FIFO) is the hallmark of Queues. Last In First Out (LIFO) is the hallmark of Stacks. Lookup Operation (efficient key-based search) is the primary operation associated with Hash Tables.

keywords.explain: The element inserted first is the one removed first, like a queue of people waiting in line.
keywords.example: In a printer queue, the first document sent to print is the first one printed.

keywords.explain: The most recently inserted element is the first to be removed, like a stack of plates.
keywords.example: Function call stack: the most recently called function returns first.

keywords.explain: Searching for an element by its key in O(1) average time, which is the defining operation of Hash Tables.
keywords.example: Looking up a word in a dictionary implemented as a hash table is O(1) average.

understand.visual_alt: Three data structures: Queue uses FIFO (front removal, back insertion). Stack uses LIFO (top insertion and removal). Hash Table uses key-based lookup with O(1) average time.

given.aim: Match each behavioral property to its corresponding data structure.

terms.meaning: Data structure where elements are added at rear and removed from front — FIFO order.
terms.example: OS process scheduling queues follow FIFO.
terms.connects: Maps to property (p): First In First Out.

terms.meaning: Data structure where elements are added and removed from the same end (top) — LIFO order.
terms.example: Undo/redo operations in text editors use stacks.
terms.connects: Maps to property (r): Last In First Out.

terms.meaning: Data structure using a hash function to map keys to buckets, enabling O(1) average-case search.
terms.example: Python dictionaries are implemented as hash tables.

given.plan: Match each property to the data structure it best describes: FIFO -> Queue, LIFO -> Stack, Lookup -> Hash Table.

steps.title: Match FIFO to Queue
steps.apply: First In First Out means the element that has been in the structure the longest is served next. This is exactly how a Queue operates: enqueue at rear, dequeue from front. So (p) FIFO -> (ii) Queues.
steps.note: Queues are used in BFS, OS scheduling, and network packet handling.

steps.title: Match LIFO to Stack
steps.apply: Last In First Out means the most recently added element is removed first. This is exactly how a Stack operates: push and pop both happen at the top. So (r) LIFO -> (i) Stacks.
steps.note: Stacks are used in DFS, expression evaluation, and function call management.

steps.title: Match Lookup Operation to Hash Tables
steps.apply: Hash Tables are designed for efficient key-based lookup using a hash function. The primary operation is search/lookup. So (q) Lookup Operation -> (iii) Hash Tables.
steps.note: While stacks and queues support lookup theoretically, Hash Tables are defined by their fast lookup capability.

steps.title: Select matching answer
steps.apply: Combined: (p)-(ii), (q)-(iii), (r)-(i). This corresponds to option A.
steps.note: This is the standard textbook mapping of these three data structure properties.

questions.formula_note: Fundamental data structure properties: Queue=FIFO, Stack=LIFO, Hash Table=O(1) average lookup via hash function.

questions.question:
An advantage of chained hash table (external hashing) over the open addressing scheme is:

A. Worst case complexity of search operations is less
B. Space used is less
C. Deletion is easier
D. None of the above
questions.to_find: The key advantage of chaining (external hashing) over open addressing for hash tables.

understand.plain: There are two main collision resolution strategies in hash tables: chaining (each bucket holds a linked list of all elements that hash to it) and open addressing (on collision, probe to find next available slot in the same array). The question asks what chaining does better than open addressing.

keywords.explain: Each hash table slot holds a linked list (chain) of all elements that hash to that slot.
keywords.example: If h(k)=3 for keys 15, 25, 35, all three are stored in a linked list at slot 3.

keywords.explain: On collision, the algorithm probes other slots in the table (linear, quadratic, or double hashing) until an empty slot is found.
keywords.example: Linear probing: if slot h(k) is occupied, try h(k)+1, h(k)+2, ... until empty slot found.

keywords.explain: Deleting an element in open addressing requires placing a special 'deleted' marker (tombstone) rather than simply removing it, because removing it would break probe sequences for other elements.
keywords.example: If key A and key B both hash to slot 3 and B is at slot 4 (probed), deleting A at slot 3 by setting it empty would make B unfindable.

understand.visual_alt: Chaining: array of buckets, each with a linked list. Open addressing: flat array where collisions probe to next slots. Deletion in chaining: simply remove the node from the linked list. Deletion in open addressing: must use tombstone markers.

given.aim: Identify the true advantage of chaining over open addressing.

terms.meaning: In chaining, worst case is O(n) when all elements hash to same slot. In open addressing, also O(n). No improvement for chaining here.
terms.example: Both can degrade to O(n) in worst case.

terms.meaning: Chaining uses extra memory for linked list pointers. Open addressing only needs the table array. Chaining uses MORE space.
terms.example: Each node in the chain needs a 'next' pointer, adding overhead.

terms.meaning: In chaining, deletion is simply removing a node from a linked list — straightforward O(1) after finding the node. In open addressing, deletion requires tombstone markers to not break probe chains.
terms.example: Remove key 25 from the chain at slot 3: just unlink the node. In open addressing, must mark the slot as 'deleted' not 'empty'.
terms.connects: Confirms option C as the correct advantage.

given.plan: Evaluate each option: worst case search is equally bad for both; space is worse for chaining; deletion is genuinely simpler in chaining because no tombstone markers are needed.

steps.title: Evaluate option A: Worst case search complexity
steps.apply: In chaining: if all n elements hash to the same bucket, search degenerates to O(n) linked list traversal. In open addressing: similarly, worst case probe length is O(n). Neither has a strictly better worst case. Option A is FALSE.
steps.note: Both chaining and open addressing have O(n) worst case for search.

steps.title: Evaluate option B: Space used is less
steps.apply: Chaining requires additional memory for linked list node pointers (overhead per stored element). Open addressing uses only the preallocated table. Chaining uses MORE space overall, not less. Option B is FALSE.
steps.note: Chaining's space advantage is that the table never needs to be resized for load factor > 1, but total memory is higher due to pointers.

steps.title: Evaluate option C: Deletion is easier
steps.apply: In chaining: to delete a key, find it in the linked list and simply remove/unlink the node. This is a standard O(1) (after O(k) search in the chain of length k) linked-list deletion — clean and complete. In open addressing: deletion is tricky because removing an element can break the probe sequence for elements inserted after it. To avoid this, a special tombstone (deleted marker) must be placed, which complicates future insertions and searches. Option C is TRUE.
steps.note: Tombstone markers in open addressing accumulate over time and can degrade performance, requiring periodic rehashing.

questions.formula_note: Chaining vs. open addressing trade-offs: chaining simplifies deletion (no tombstones needed), but uses more memory due to pointer overhead; worst-case search is O(n) for both.

questions.question:
Insert the characters of the string K R P C S N Y T J M into a hash table of size 10. Use the hash function

h(x) = (ord(x) - ord('a') + 1) mod 10

A. Which insertions cause collisions?
B. Display the final hash table.
questions.answer: B. Display the final hash table
questions.to_find: Which insertions cause collisions and the final state of the hash table after inserting K R P C S N Y T J M using the given hash function with linear probing.

understand.plain: We use a hash function based on the position of a character in the alphabet. For uppercase letters, we compute ord(x) - ord('a') + 1, but since these are uppercase, it's likely ord(x) - ord('A') + 1. We insert 10 characters into a size-10 table using open addressing with linear probing, and need to identify which insertions cause collisions.

keywords.explain: Maps a character to a bucket index using its alphabet position modulo table size.
When the computed slot is occupied, try slot+1, slot+2, ... (wrapping around) until an empty slot is found.
keywords.example: If slot 1 is full, try slot 2, then 3, etc.

keywords.explain: Two keys map to the same hash slot, requiring probing to find an alternate slot.
keywords.example: If both K and A hash to slot 1, inserting the second causes a collision.

understand.visual_alt: Hash table insertion trace for K R P C S N Y T J M with h(x)=(ord(x)-ord('A')+1) mod 10 and linear probing.

given.aim: Insert 10 characters into a hash table of size 10 using h(x) = (ord(x) - ord('a') + 1) mod 10 with linear probing, identify collisions and final table.

terms.term: String: K R P C S N Y T J M
terms.meaning: The 10 characters to insert, treated as uppercase letters.
terms.example: K is the 11th letter, R is 18th, P is 16th, etc.
terms.connects: Each character's alphabet position determines its hash value.

terms.meaning: Hash function mapping character x to a slot 0-9. For uppercase: ord('A')=65 so h(X) = (ord(X) - 64) mod 10.
terms.connects: Gives the initial slot for each character.

terms.meaning: Collision resolution: try slot h(x), h(x)+1, h(x)+2, ... mod table_size until empty.
terms.example: If slot 0 is full, try slot 1; if slot 1 is also full, try slot 2.
terms.connects: Determines final placement when collisions occur.

given.plan: Compute h(x) for each character using ord(x) - ord('A') + 1, then insert in order using linear probing, tracking which insertions find the computed slot already occupied.

steps.title: Compute hash values for each character
Insert characters sequentially with linear probing
steps.formula_raw: If slot h(x) occupied, try (h(x)+1) mod 10, (h(x)+2) mod 10, ...
steps.apply:
K -> h=1, slot 1 empty -> place at 1. Table: [_,K,_,_,_,_,_,_,_,_]
R -> h=8, slot 8 empty -> place at 8. Table: [_,K,_,_,_,_,_,_,R,_]
P -> h=6, slot 6 empty -> place at 6. Table: [_,K,_,_,_,_,P,_,R,_]
C -> h=3, slot 3 empty -> place at 3. Table: [_,K,_,C,_,_,P,_,R,_]
S -> h=9, slot 9 empty -> place at 9. Table: [_,K,_,C,_,_,P,_,R,S]
N -> h=4, slot 4 empty -> place at 4. Table: [_,K,_,C,N,_,P,_,R,S]
Y -> h=5, slot 5 empty -> place at 5. Table: [_,K,_,C,N,Y,P,_,R,S]
T -> h=0, slot 0 empty -> place at 0. Table: [T,K,_,C,N,Y,P,_,R,S]
J -> h=0, slot 0 OCCUPIED (T) -> COLLISION. Try slot 1: occupied (K) -> try slot 2: empty -> place J at 2. Table: [T,K,J,C,N,Y,P,_,R,S]
M -> h=3, slot 3 OCCUPIED (C) -> COLLISION. Try slot 4: occupied (N) -> try slot 5: occupied (Y) -> try slot 6: occupied (P) -> try slot 7: empty -> place M at 7. Table: [T,K,J,C,N,Y,P,M,R,S]
steps.note: J causes 1 collision (slot 0 taken by T). M causes multiple collisions (slots 3,4,5,6 all taken).

steps.title: Identify all collisions
steps.apply:
Insertions that caused collisions:
- J (h=0): collided with T at slot 0, and K at slot 1, placed at slot 2.
- M (h=3): collided with C at slot 3, N at 4, Y at 5, P at 6, placed at slot 7.
All other insertions (K, R, P, C, S, N, Y, T) had no collisions.
steps.note: Two out of ten insertions caused collisions: J and M.

solution.result: Collisions occur for J (hashes to slot 0, placed at slot 2) and M (hashes to slot 3, placed at slot 7). Final table: [T, K, J, C, N, Y, P, M, R, S] at slots [0..9].

questions.formula_note: Open addressing with linear probing: h(x) = (ord(x) - ord('a') + 1) mod table_size; on collision probe linearly until empty slot found.
